name: Boudin Du Pays (Blood Pudding)
recipeInstructions:
Blood pudding is one of the great delicacies of Acadian cuisine. It used to be that every Acadian family made its own. Since the annual slaughter came during Advent, the boudin was usually saved for the Christmas holidays. Also part of Cajun cuisine.
1. When slaughtering a pig, collect the fresh blood immediately and add salt to prevent coagulation.
2. Cut the fresh pork, lung, heart, and neck into large pieces. Place the meat in a large pot with just enough water to cover it. Add the salt and chopped onions. Simmer on medium heat for 3 hours. Remove the meat from the cooking liquid and let it cool.
3. Cut the meat into very small pieces or grind it with a meat grinder. Add the meat, remaining onions, pepper, and spices back to the cooking liquid. Bring the mixture to a boil and slowly add the blood through a sieve. Stir constantly.
4. Add the flour mixed with a small amount of water (the flour may be browned in the oven before adding it to the meat). Simmer the mixture on low heat for about 1 hour, stirring frequently.
5. This sauce can be warmed in a skillet when ready to serve.
To make blood pudding sausages:
1. Prepare the blood pudding sauce without simmering for the last half hour.
2. Clean small intestines of the pig and cut them into 20-inch pieces. Tie one end.
3. Fill the intestinal lining with the sauce until it is three quarters full, using a funnel or birch bark as tradition dictates.
4. Press out any air and tie the other end, leaving space for expansion.
5. Cook the sausages in boiling water for 45 to 1 hour.
